Now there are humorous writers, so Woody Allen was a stand-up comic, Woody Allen can write funny. If you read Woody Allen, you laugh. If he appeals to you at all. But, and you go back in time benchly, these people wrote funny. But most stand-up comedy is not funny if you take yourself out of the environment in which you and everyone around you is in the state of fun and you read it or even have it read to you. Not really funny. The states very important that they are in fun to start with. In environments like a seminar with your own troops, so speakers get lulled into the idea that they're funny because everybody laughs at them when they do their jokes in the seminar room and some of them think they can actually go do stand-up comedy and they come back with their heads severed because the audience of their fans in the seminar room is really in a state of go along with anything and that's not the state they're in out there. So what we actually want is a state of gullibility. Now again that's why so much opportunity and a lot of improvement over the years and a lot of personal development has been sold in environments where you could create a certain state that distance makes it very difficult to do. I'm going to talk later about the big bugaboo of length of presentation and nobody will read anything and nobody will listen to anything and nobody will watch anything so we got to shrink everything down to a short pre-state. Worst thing in the world you can do. Your only prayer at a distance is to deliver so much that they get themselves into a state while they are going through it and experiencing it. I hope you got. So the idea that you're going to shrink all your messages and everything you deliver people down to something that'll fit in 146 characters you're just committing suicide because they can't get in state and we need them in a particular state. Ideally we need them in a state of gullibility. So what does gullibility mean? The off chance that you might not go look it up for yourself on page 73 I have provided you with some definitions. Unsuspecting, unsuspicious, unweary, overtrusting, overtrustful. So what we're looking for is a scenario in which our prospect is over trusting of us which means we have to establish certain things about ourselves. He will be easily persuaded so he is willing to take this whole, he's willing to tell his conscious mind to go take a hike. He's willing to take all this stuff, set it aside and buy this. All right. We need to create a scenario therefore in which his vulnerabilities and desires can be exploited because really that's how you overcome all that stuff. So why does somebody buy a lottery today? Is there anybody here who plays a lottery every week? That's all right, admit it. Come on. Really nobody? We'll see you. Look, you got to make a bet at least once a day. You could be walking around lucky and not know it. I mean, you know, well, see you're lucky just because you got here. Now you see you got to make a bet every day, you got to. You could be lucky and not know it. All right. And what's the lottery slogan? Can't win if you don't play. All right. Now how many people do you think buy a lottery ticket who have any serious thought that they are going to win the lottery? A ton. Exactly. Even though the reason you don't buy it is why. Well, yeah, because you feel like an ass, right? You don't buy it because you feel stupid. Because you know the odds are, I mean, we know, you know, your odds are worse than being hit four times by lightning while sitting in this room on a sunny day next to a tall person with metal teeth. I mean, you know, you it just can't happen. Right. I mean, yes, somebody wins, but you know, so you don't buy tickets. But the vast majority of people who buy tickets are so gullible about that at that moment that they actually think one of these days they're going to hit this thing. Most buyers do what? How do they play the lottery? Thank you. Same numbers. They play the same numbers. Like that makes a difference. Okay. So Pete little I go gambling. So here's something. So Pete has a belief. Okay, that and sometimes his experience validates it, which makes it worse. All right. So he has a belief about slot machines. You cash out and start over because the machine is more generous to a new player than it is to a player that has been there for a long period of time. And in fact, it knows it just gave you $1,200. So it is somehow programmed now to start getting that $1,200 back. Okay. Now, clearly this is all horseshit. Okay. Now he's got me doing it. But clearly, but really more to be sociable then. Okay. So the one desire everybody does have, for example, that plays to most of us is to be rich.
